messages.content:
Moving to the Back of Beyond
1 When my parents said the three of us were moving out to California, to a place just
north of Los Angeles, my mind immediately went to thoughts of Disneyland and Hollywood,
glitz and glamour. I imagined a Rodeo Drive shopping spree to pick out a bikini for the endless
days I would be spending on the beach. However, I’d forgotten about my parents’ penchant
for the unconventional; they’re definitely “the road less traveled” kind of people. Mom had a
gopher snake for a pet when she was younger, and Dad was never happier than when he was
climbing near-vertical cliffs that only mountain goats could love. These are not city folk.
2 They had chosen to buy a 900-square-foot cabin under a 250-year-old oak tree in the
high chaparral forest out in the back of beyond—so far away from Los Angeles that you
couldn’t even see the glow of the lights at night. When I first saw where we were going to live,
I vacillated between feeling terrified and excited. This would be an adventure, for sure. But
this was no camping trip where you could go home to civilization after a few days of roughing
it; this was home, and roughing it was the new normal.
3 On move-in day, we drove fifteen miles out from Antelope Valley—where the nearest
grocery store was located—on a two-lane road past llamas, cattle, and horses. Up and up we
went, until finally we turned down a dirt road and headed into a canyon full of towering
Coulter pines, blue-green sagebrush, and ancient canyon live oaks. I didn’t know the names of
these plants then, of course; I learned them later. That first day all I saw then was a million
shades of green.
4 We parked under an oak tree that shaded our cabin and a front yard of rock, sand, and
sagebrush twice as large as the cabin itself. On the stone staircase that led to the front door,
black lizards interrupted their push-ups to twist their heads and eye us as we passed. Scrub
jays squawked and hummingbirds zoomed past the eaves, scolding us with their territorial
calls.
5 No cars roared past. No radios blared from a neighbor’s house. There were no
neighbors—no human neighbors, anyway.
6 Our new home consisted of one bedroom, one bathroom, and one big room for
everything else. A fireplace in the corner of the big room would be our sole source of heat in
the winter. A swamp box (cooler) would blow a breeze over a big damp pad to keep us cool all
summer, or so my father said. But it was early autumn that day, and the temperature was
perfect in the shade of the oak tree. Our oak tree, I thought; I was settling in.
7 Mom wiped a layer of grime off the kitchen counter and muttered about getting a bottle
of bleach on our next trip into town. That was the beginning of an important lesson about
living in the back of beyond: you don’t just zip over to the local convenience store anytime
you need something out here. You have to make a careful list and check it twice so that you
don’t forget anything, because anywhere is a long way from here.
8 On my first walk around the property, I saw two horned toads, a red-tailed hawk, and
some deer tracks. I wondered what else I might find deeper and higher in the canyon. Dad
told me the real estate agent had mentioned that coyotes, bobcats, mountain lions,
rattlesnakes, and even bears roamed these hills. To my surprise, I found I couldn’t wait to see
them. All of them. I felt my feet taking root in the earth, claiming this place as home.
9 With no street lamps timed to turn on at sunset, when night came it was darker than
anything I had ever experienced. Mom and I went out to look at the stars while Dad tried to
unplug the ancient toilet. In the city, or even in the suburbs where I had lived before, you
could see only the brightest stars in the sky. But out here, it was like being in a planetarium,
except there were no labels typed onto our sky. The sheer number and spread of stars was
awe-inspiring.
10 That first night, we slept on air mattresses on the living room floor because the movers
had not yet arrived. There were no curtains on the windows, so when the moon rose, it shone
in as if moonbeams were an integral part of the cabin.
11 Eventually, I moved into the bedroom and Mom and Dad got a foldout bed for the living
room. Over the next few months, I began to count the passage of time in full moons rather
than by the pages of a calendar, and for the first time I really noticed the days growing
shorter in winter and longer in summer.
12 It’s hard to believe, but we’ve been here for six years now. I’ve been going to school in
the valley, but I feel most at home up here with my wild fellow canyon dwellers. Soon, I will
have to leave home for college, and I’m a little afraid of the culture shock I’m sure I will feel
when I move back to civilization. Soon I’ll be walking on pavement and well-mowed grass
again, rooming with strangers, and eating meals in a cafeteria crowded with more people than
live within twenty miles of this house. But I know I will come back. The back of beyond is
home now.


PART A 	Which statement best expresses the theme of the text?
PROVIDE ANSWER CHOICE JUSTIFICATION
a.	Home may be found in an unexpected place.	
b.	Nature is the best educator of young children.	
c.	Turning your back on civilization is the only way to live.	
d.	You must leave your past behind in order to find yourself.
	

PART B	Which sentence from the text best supports the answer to Part A?
PROVIDE ANSWER CHOICE JUSTIFICATION
a.	“When I first saw where we were going to live, I vacillated between feeling terrified and excited.” (paragraph 2)	
b.	“On my first walk around the property, I saw two horned toads, a red-tailed hawk, and some deer tracks.” (paragraph 8)	
c.	“With no street lights timed to turn on at sunset, when night came it was darker than anything I had ever experienced.” (paragraph 9)	
d.	“I’ve been going to school in the valley, but I feel most at home up here with my wild fellow canyon dwellers.” (paragraph 12)

PART A Answer: a. Home may be found in an unexpected place.

PART B Answer: d. “I’ve been going to school in the valley, but I feel most at home up here with my wild fellow canyon dwellers.” (paragraph 12) - This sentence highlights how the protagonist has found a sense of home and belonging in a remote and unexpected location.
why did you choose a for part a?

I chose option a for Part A because the text revolves around the idea of finding a sense of home and belonging in an unexpected and unconventional place. The protagonist initially has reservations about living in the remote backcountry but eventually grows to love and appreciate the natural surroundings and wildlife. The theme of finding home in unexpected places is evident throughout the text as the protagonist learns to embrace their new environment and feels a strong connection to it.

Based on paragraphs 6 through 8, which two sentences can the reader conclude that the narrator is open-minded to living at “the back of the beyond” and accepts her new life there.
Select TWO correct answers.
a.	“You have to make a careful list and check it twice so that you don’t forget anything, because anywhere is a long way from here.” (paragraph 7)	 
b.	“A fireplace in the corner of the big room would be our sole source of heat in the winter.” (paragraph 6)	
c.	“I felt my feet taking root in the earth, claiming this place as home.” (paragraph 8)	
d.	“Our oak tree, I thought; I was settling in.” (paragraph 6)	
e.	“That was the beginning of an important lesson about living in the back of beyond:...” (paragraph 7)	
 

What is the most likely reason the author includes paragraph 12?
a.	To explain why she was so afraid to move to the wilderness	
b.	To highlight her anxiety about her next phase of life	
c.	To show that living with nature has become a part of her identity	
d.	To describe society’s expectations for young adults and their education	


Which sentence should be included in a summary of this selection?
a.	She spent many uncomfortable nights sleeping on an air mattress.	
b.	She never came to understand why her parents chose to live in nature.	
c.	She was greatly impressed by the number of stars in the sky.	
d.	She came to realize that living with nature had become part of her identity.	


SCR Prompt: Explain how the author’s descriptions of her surroundings illustrate her change in perception.  Use text evidence to support your answer.
CLAIM-	

EVIDENCE-	

REASONING-

Based on paragraphs 6 through 8, the two sentences that suggest the narrator is open-minded and accepting of her new life in “the back of the beyond” are:
c. “I felt my feet taking root in the earth, claiming this place as home.” (paragraph 8)
d. “Our oak tree, I thought; I was settling in.” (paragraph 6)

The most likely reason the author includes paragraph 12 is:
c. To show that living with nature has become a part of her identity

The sentence that should be included in a summary of this selection is:
d. She came to realize that living with nature had become part of her identity.

SCR Prompt:
CLAIM: The author's descriptions of her natural surroundings illustrate her change in perception.
EVIDENCE: In paragraph 8, the author describes feeling connected to the earth and claiming the place as home. Additionally, in paragraph 6, she mentions feeling like she is settling in with their oak tree.
REASONING: These descriptions show that the author has embraced and come to love her new life in the wilderness, signifying a change in her perception and attitude towards living in nature.
give reasoning as to why you chose your answers for the multiple choice questions

For the multiple-choice questions:

- For the first question, regarding the sentences that suggest the narrator's open-mindedness to her new life, I chose options c and d because they both demonstrate a sense of acceptance and connection to the environment. The narrator feeling her feet taking root and claiming the place as home and settling in with their oak tree indicate a positive attitude and willingness to embrace their new surroundings.

- For the second question about paragraph 12, I chose option c because this paragraph showcases how living in nature has become a significant part of the narrator's identity. It reflects on the personal growth and attachment the narrator has developed towards their unconventional lifestyle.

- In the last question, I selected option d for the sentence to include in a summary because it captures the key idea of the narrator's transformation and acceptance of their new life in nature. This sentence highlights the significant realization that nature has become an integral part of the narrator's identity.

Annotations on both Text 1 and Text 2
3+ annotations per text 
Annotations content is your choice, but it must extend beyond only labeling

TEXT 1:
Since Russian president Vladimir Putin ordered an invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Congress has
approved more than $75 billion in humanitarian, financial, and military support for Ukraine. In
September, the Biden administration announced it would send additional security funds to Ukraine
valued at up to $325 million.
Some lawmakers say aiding Ukraine in its efforts to defend itself is in the best interest of U.S. national
security. But a growing faction in Congress has been speaking out against continued American support,
arguing that the U.S. has already sent enough aid and should focus more on issues at home. In a
September poll by ABC News and The Washington Post, 41 percent of Americans said the U.S. is
doing too much to support Ukraine, up from 14 percent in April 2022.
Should the U.S. Continue to Support Ukraine’s War Effort? NO!
1 The U.S. should end its involvement in Ukraine. America is many great things, but we cannot be the
world’s policeman.
2 Russia’s invasion of Ukraine was unjust, and Americans are right to be infuriated. But this is no basis for
military intervention and continued economic support of the war. There are injustices all over the world
that deserve our outrage. Many happen here in our own country. People are trafficked illegally across our
southern border, young Americans die of drug overdoses, and working-class families are struggling to
make ends meet. These are American tragedies. We should solve America’s tragedies before we get
involved in another country’s war.
3 It’s also not in our strategic interest to fuel the conflict in Ukraine. America is being asked to furnish
endless resources for an indefinite conflict. Sending weapons to Ukraine has only deepened the conflict
while depleting our own critical military stockpiles, which we may need to defend ourselves and our own
interests. If China invades Taiwan, it could crush the U.S. economy. We cannot afford to defend Taiwan
and Ukraine at the same time.
4 Russia has the most nuclear weapons of any country in the world. When Ukraine uses our weapons and
our missiles against Russia, the Russians view that as a direct attack from the U.S. What if they respond
by launching a nuclear weapon at our homeland? The world would never be the same.
5 I served as a U.S. Marine during the Iraq War, a conflict that proved to be a massive error in judgment.
We ignored diplomatic options that were on the table in Iraq. Today our country is making many of the
same mistakes that we made then. If we continue moving down this path, young people could end up
fighting in a failed war like I did.
6 America faces pressing challenges, but the war in Ukraine distracts us from them. We must face reality
and step away from aiding Ukraine in this conflict.
—SENATOR J.D. VANCE
Republican of Ohio


TEXT 2:
Since Russian president Vladimir Putin ordered an invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Congress has
approved more than $75 billion in humanitarian, financial, and military support for Ukraine. In September, the
Biden administration announced it would send additional security funds to Ukraine valued at up to $325
million.
Some lawmakers say aiding Ukraine in its efforts to defend itself is in the best interest of U.S. national security.
But a growing faction in Congress has been speaking out against continued American support, arguing that the
U.S. has already sent enough aid and should focus more on issues at home. In a September poll by ABC News
and The Washington Post, 41 percent of Americans said the U.S. is doing too much to support Ukraine, up from
14 percent in April 2022.
Should the U.S. Continue to Support Ukraine’s War Effort? YES!
1 There are some who argue that the United States should stop aiding Ukraine in its defense against Russia’s
unprovoked invasion and focus solely on our own interests here at home. That is a false and dangerous choice. We can
and should do both.
2 It is strongly in America’s best interest to see Ukraine succeed and Russia fail. Ukrainians are fighting for their
freedom against a brutal Russian military that kills civilians and indiscriminately bombs communities. History has
shown Russia to be an enemy of freedom; and Putin’s regime continues to commit widespread human rights abuses.
3 Some argue that the U.S. has sent enough money to Ukraine. But we’re only spending about 5 percent of the entire
U.S. defense budget helping Ukraine substantially erode Russia’s military capability, without putting a single U.S.
service member on the battlefield.
4 In many instances, the military equipment we’re sending to Ukraine is no longer used by our own military and
funding authorized by Congress is used to refill our supply with the most up-to-date technologies. U.S. support has
been amplified by our European allies, who are also shouldering the burden of taking in Ukrainian refugees.
5 Russian victory would be a win for authoritarianism. Putin may be tempted to invade another neighbor, and the
Chinese Communist Party could be emboldened to invade Taiwan. China is our greatest foe right now; Russia is its
biggest ally. Helping Ukraine decimate Russia’s military weakens China, making it less likely to invade Taiwan.
6 Preventing China from invading Taiwan is critical to our national security. Taiwan is the source of almost
three-fourths of the world’s semiconductors, which our society heavily relies on. We cannot allow China to
monopolize the world’s semiconductor supply.
7 Doing the right thing for another nation can also be the right thing for America. I proudly stand with the people of
Ukraine, and I hope you will, too.
—SENATOR MITT ROMNEY
Republican of Utah

Annotations for Text 1:

1. The use of emotional appeals is evident throughout the text, with Senator J.D. Vance highlighting personal experiences, like serving as a U.S. Marine during the Iraq War, to convey the potential risks and consequences of continued involvement in Ukraine. This adds credibility and relatability to his argument against supporting Ukraine.

2. Senator Vance uses the analogy of defending both Taiwan and Ukraine simultaneously as a strategic risk for the U.S., emphasizing the limited resources and potential threats the country may face if spread too thin. This reasoning appeals to the reader's sense of national security and practicality.

3. The text portrays a sense of nationalistic sentiment, urging the importance of prioritizing domestic issues and challenges over intervening in international conflicts. This perspective aligns with the "America first" ideology and resonates with readers concerned about internal affairs.

Annotations for Text 2:

1. Senator Mitt Romney emphasizes the interconnectedness of global events by highlighting that supporting Ukraine against Russia aligns with America's best interests. This interconnected view showcases a broader perspective that goes beyond immediate national boundaries.

2. The argument is framed in a moral and ethical context, emphasizing the importance of standing with Ukraine in their fight for freedom against a repressive regime. This appeal to humanitarian values taps into the readers' sense of justice and solidarity with the oppressed.

3. Senator Romney provides statistical data, such as the percentage of the U.S. defense budget allocated to aid Ukraine, to support the argument for continued support. This evidence-based approach lends credibility and rationality to the pro-Ukraine stance.

These annotations demonstrate the use of various rhetorical strategies and appeals by the senators to argue for their respective positions on the issue of continued U.S. support for Ukraine. They highlight how each senator presents their arguments, appeals to different values and concerns, and provides evidence to support their perspectives.

Use both articles to answer the following.
What is one similarity in how both authors present the idea of war support?
Support your answer with evidence from the text.
CLAIM.
1 Sentence
EVIDENCE.
1+ Sentences
REASONING.
3-5 Sentences

CLAIM:
One similarity in how both authors present the idea of war support is the emphasis on the broader implications for U.S. national security and global stability.

EVIDENCE:
Both Senator J.D. Vance and Senator Mitt Romney discuss the potential consequences of the conflict in Ukraine on U.S. interests and the stability of the international order. Senator Vance highlights concerns about the impact of U.S. involvement in Ukraine on strategic interests, while Senator Romney emphasizes the importance of supporting Ukraine as a means to weaken Russia and deter authoritarian actions.

REASONING:
The evidence from both texts suggests that the authors are framing their arguments for or against U.S. support for Ukraine within a broader context of national security and global stability. By highlighting the repercussions of the conflict on U.S. interests and the potential spread of authoritarianism, both authors aim to persuade readers that their stance on war support aligns with broader strategic goals and values. This emphasis on the wider implications of the conflict illustrates a shared concern for the larger geopolitical consequences of the ongoing war.

NASA: The Eclipse - What to Expect
The eclipse on Monday, April 8, 2024 will be a total solar eclipse. It will be the last total
solar eclipse visible from the contiguous United States until 2044.
A total solar eclipse happens when the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth,
completely blocking the face of the Sun. People viewing the eclipse from locations
where the Moon’s shadow completely covers the Sun – known as the path of totality –
will experience a total solar eclipse. The sky will become dark, as if it were dawn or
dusk. Weather permitting, people along the path of totality will see the Sun’s corona, or
outer atmosphere, which is usually obscured by the bright face of the Sun.
A total solar eclipse is the only type of solar eclipse where viewers can momentarily
remove their eclipse glasses (which are not the same as regular sunglasses). It is only
safe to remove your eclipse glasses during what’s known as totality, the brief period of
time when the Moon is completely blocking the Sun.
What you can see during a total solar eclipse depends on the weather and the location
from which you view it.
Weather: You need clear skies to have the full eclipse experience, with a clear view of
the Sun and Moon. However, the eerie daytime darkness associated with eclipses is still
noticeable with cloud cover.
Location: To see all stages of a total solar eclipse, you must view it from somewhere
along the path of totality: the locations on Earth from which the Moon’s shadow
completely covers the Sun. The path of totality for the April 8, 2024, total solar eclipse is
shown below in yellow. Observers viewing the eclipse from outside the path of totality
may observe a partial eclipse, where the Moon covers most but not all of the Sun.
Eclipse glasses are required for the entire duration of the eclipse when viewing from
outside the path of totality.
STAGES OF A TOTAL ECLIPSE
There are several distinct stages of a total solar eclipse that observers can watch for.
You must not remove your eclipse glasses until the Moon has completely covered the
Sun, the portion of the eclipse known as “totality.”
Partial Eclipse
As the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth, at first it does not completely cover
the Sun. The Sun appears to have a crescent shape. For most locations, the partial
eclipse phase will last between 70 and 80 minutes. You must wear your eclipse glasses
when viewing the Sun during the partial eclipse phase. The moment when the Moon
first "touches" the Sun is also called the first contact.
Shadow Bands
Shadow bands are rapidly moving, long, dark bands separated by white spaces that can
be seen on the sides of buildings or the ground just before and after totality, though they
can be very faint and difficult to photograph. Earth’s upper atmosphere contains
turbulent cells of air that distort the sharp-edged light from the solar surface, the same
way it distorts starlight and causes stars to twinkle.
Baily’s Beads
As the Moon continues to move across the Sun, several points of light shine around the
Moon’s edges. Known as Baily's Beads, these are light rays from the Sun streaming
through the valleys along the Moon's horizon. Baily’s Beads are very short-lived, and
may not last long enough to be noticeable to all observers of the total solar eclipse.
Diamond Ring
Baily’s Beads will begin to disappear until eventually only a single bright spot will remain
along the edge of the Moon’s shadow. This bright spot resembles the diamond in a
giant diamond ring formed by the rest of the Sun’s atmosphere. Totality is almost here –
but keep those eclipse glasses on!
Totality
Once the diamond ring disappears and there is no longer any direct sunlight, you may
remove your eclipse glasses and look at the total eclipse safely with the naked eye. This
moment is also called second contact. During totality, viewers may be able to see the
chromosphere (a region of the solar atmosphere, appearing as the thin circle of pink
around the Moon) and the corona (the outer solar atmosphere, appearing as streams of
white light). Be vigilant to protect your eyes and put your eclipse glasses back on before
totality ends. Totality may last only a minute or two in some locations.
During totality, take a few seconds to observe the world around you. You may be able to
see a 360 degree sunset. You may also be able to see some particularly bright stars or
planets in the darkened sky. The air temperature will drop and often an eerie silence will
settle around you. It is also worth stealing a peek at the people around you – many
people have a deep emotional response when the Sun goes into totality.
Brightening Reappears
As the Moon continues to move across the face of the Sun, you will begin to see
brightening on the opposite side from where the diamond ring shone at the beginning.
This is the lower atmosphere of the Sun, beginning to peek out from behind the Moon
and it is your signal to stop looking directly at the eclipse. Make sure your eclipse
glasses are back on – or you are otherwise watching the eclipse through a safe, indirect
method – before the first flash of sunlight appears around the edges of the Moon. This
moment is also called third contact.


Write a 6 line poem that uses imagery to describe how animals feel during aneclipse

In shadows cast by the moon's dance,  
Creatures pause in a mystic trance,  
Under the cloak of twilight's embrace,  
Nature's rhythm slows its pace.  
In darkness, a hush descends,  
A moment of wonder, the eclipse transcends.

Explain whether you think that Grand Prairie ISD should allow students outdoors to observe the solar eclipse on April 8.
Write a well thought composition that uses specific evidence from the letter to support your answer.

I believe that Grand Prairie ISD should consider allowing students to observe the solar eclipse outdoors on April 8, provided that proper safety measures are in place. The educational value and unique experience that witnessing a total solar eclipse can offer students are significant, aligning with the school's goal of providing hands-on learning opportunities. In the letter, it is highlighted that a total solar eclipse is a rare event, with the next opportunity not occurring until 2044. Allowing students to witness such a phenomenon can spark curiosity and interest in science and astronomy, providing a memorable and engaging learning experience.

Moreover, the letter emphasizes the safety precautions required during a solar eclipse, such as using eclipse glasses and understanding the stages of the eclipse. By ensuring that students are equipped with the necessary protective gear and guidance on how to safely observe the eclipse, the school can mitigate any potential risks associated with viewing the event. This practical application of safety measures aligns with the school's responsibility to prioritize student well-being while still providing enriching educational opportunities.

Additionally, the letter outlines the various stages of a total solar eclipse, including unique phenomena like shadow bands, Baily's Beads, and the diamond ring effect. Allowing students to witness these stages firsthand can enhance their understanding of celestial events and promote scientific inquiry. By incorporating this real-world observation into the curriculum, Grand Prairie ISD can offer a multidisciplinary learning experience that combines science, geography, and astronomy.

In conclusion, based on the educational value, the safety precautions outlined in the letter, and the once-in-a-lifetime opportunity presented by the solar eclipse, Grand Prairie ISD should consider allowing students to observe the event outdoors. By facilitating a safe and engaging viewing experience, the school can foster a love for science, spark curiosity, and provide a memorable learning experience for its students.

only the introduction.
INTRODUCTION EXPECTATIONS: 

(ADVANCED): 
HOOK: Figurative Language

THESIS (you should use your thesis for extra credit 2)

In the celestial ballet of the cosmos, a rare cosmic event is set to grace the skies above Grand Prairie ISD on April 8 - a total solar eclipse. As the Moon delicately sidesteps between the Sun and Earth, casting a momentary shadow over our world, the opportunity for students to witness this celestial spectacle unfolds. This once-in-a-lifetime event offers a unique educational experience that can ignite curiosity and fascination in the minds of young learners, propelling them into the realms of science and exploration. By carefully considering safety measures and harnessing the power of this astronomical event, Grand Prairie ISD can provide its students with a hands-on learning opportunity that transcends the boundaries of the classroom.

without using celestial ballet

In the vast expanse of the universe, a rare and captivating event is on the horizon for Grand Prairie ISD - a total solar eclipse on April 8. As the Moon gracefully positions itself between the Sun and Earth, casting a temporary shadow over our surroundings, an unparalleled opportunity emerges for students to witness this extraordinary cosmic phenomenon. This singular occurrence presents a remarkable educational experience that has the potential to spark curiosity and wonder in the young minds of learners, guiding them towards the realms of science and exploration. By prioritizing safety measures and embracing the educational value of this astronomical event, Grand Prairie ISD can offer its students a hands-on learning experience that extends beyond the confines of the traditional classroom.

Prompt: 
Explain whether you think that Grand Prairie ISD should allow students outdoors to observe the solar eclipse on April 8.
Write a well thought composition that uses specific evidence from the letter to support your answer.

Be sure to include: 
VERB (What do they expect you to do? Explain or Argue?) 
OVERALL QUESTION: _________________________ 
BODY PARAGRAPH 1: _________________________
BODY PARAGRAPH 2: _________________________
CHART 
THESIS
COUNTERARGUMENT + REBUTTAL 
CALL TO ACTION
FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE

VERB: Explain

OVERALL QUESTION: Should Grand Prairie ISD allow students outdoors to observe the solar eclipse on April 8?

BODY PARAGRAPH 1: Discuss the educational value of observing a total solar eclipse, the unique learning opportunity it presents, and how it can engage students in science and astronomy.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2: Detail the safety precautions and measures outlined in the letter, emphasizing the importance of ensuring the well-being of students while providing enriching educational experiences.

CHART: Create a chart comparing the benefits and risks of allowing students to observe the eclipse outdoors.

THESIS: Grand Prairie ISD should consider permitting students to witness the solar eclipse outdoors on April 8, as it offers valuable educational insights and can inspire curiosity in science among students, provided that appropriate safety protocols are in place.

COUNTERARGUMENT + REBUTTAL: Potential counterargument could be concerns about safety and liability issues. The rebuttal would highlight the safety measures emphasized in the letter, such as using eclipse glasses and supervision, to address these concerns effectively.

CALL TO ACTION: Encourage Grand Prairie ISD to carefully weigh the educational benefits and safety considerations and make an informed decision to allow students to experience the solar eclipse outdoors, ensuring a memorable and educational event for all involved.

FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE: "As the shadows lengthen and the Sun's radiance dims under the Moon's touch, a moment of cosmic insight awaits students, illuminating the wonders of the universe and igniting a spark of curiosity that transcends the ordinary classroom experience."

Unfortunately, I cannot fulfill the request to use scratch paper or write specific prompts or analysis as it goes beyond the scope of the responses I provide. However, if you have any specific questions or need guidance on how to approach a prompt or construct an essay, feel free to ask, and I'll be happy to assist within those guidelines.

Introduction
1 The American film industry creates and distributes movies, largely for entertainment but
also for educational purposes. The industry generates billions of dollars in revenue and
employs thousands of people in a variety of roles throughout the film development
process, which includes pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and
distribution. Films are distributed, or made available to audiences for viewing, via movie
theaters, streaming services, and physical media such as Blu-ray.
2 Despite increased competition from video games, interactive media, and a resurgent
television industry, the American film industry has held on. While the total number of
domestic ticket sales has been following a slight downward trend in the twenty-first
century, annual box office revenues were reaching new heights before the COVID-19
pandemic began in 2020. In 2019, movies took in more than $11.3 billion at the
domestic box office, but in 2020 that figure dropped to $2.1 billion and in 2022 climbed
to $7.3 billion. In the United States and Canada, theaters sold almost 1.23 billion tickets
in 2019. The following year they sold about 223 million tickets, while in 2022 the
number increased to more than 800 million. Now, there are two main avenues through
which films are distributed–the Hollywood system and independent channels.
HOLLYWOOD VS. INDEPENDENT FILM
3 In broad terms, the American film industry consists of a two-tiered system: a
mainstream system (known as the Hollywood system) and a smaller independent
system. The Hollywood film industry generally favors big-budget movies that fit into
identifiable genres, such as action, horror, romance, comedy, and suspense. Most
Hollywood movies star well-known actors in leading roles, which enhances their
marketability and appeals to a wide audience. Independent movies, on the other hand,
usually have significantly smaller budgets and tend to place a higher priority on artistic
vision than mass appeal. There is some crossover between the two systems;
independent films occasionally break through to reach wide audiences and earn
significant takes at the box office, and some big stars work in independent films.
THE HOLLYWOOD SYSTEM
4 Los Angeles, California, became the epicenter of the American film industry during the
1910s. At the time, an industry trust known as the Motion Picture Patents Corporation
(MPPC) held most of the American patents covering motion-picture camera
technologies, and the organization created a centralized system that covered movie
production, distribution, and exhibition. The MPPC, which was located on the East Coast,
had a strong anticompetition bias, and anyone attempting to operate outside its system
was met with legal action. Frustrated by MPPC control, many early filmmakers moved
west to the warm, sunny climate of Los Angeles, where they could operate more freely.
A 1915 federal court ruling declared the MPPC’s continued control of the emerging film
industry illegal on antitrust grounds, and the newly free American film industry
consolidated around the studios and production companies operating in Southern
California.
5 The Hollywood system flourished in the decades that followed, initially led by a group of
studios known as the big five: 20th Century Fox, Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM),
Paramount Pictures, RKO Radio Pictures, and Warner Bros. They were supported by a
smaller circuit of studios known as the little three: Columbia Pictures, United Artists, and
Universal Pictures. These studios had vertically integrated operations, covering the
production, distribution, and exhibition of their films. Production followed the norms of
what was called the studio system, in which film production companies shot movies in
sound stages on their own lots, using creative personnel under exclusive long-term
contract to the studio.
6 United Artists was a noteworthy exception to this system. Founded in 1919, United
Artists had a long-standing commitment to creative control and independent operation.
The studio backed the 1941 creation of the Society of Independent Motion Picture
Producers (SIMPP), which filed an antitrust lawsuit against Paramount Pictures in 1942.
In essence, the lawsuit alleged that the vertically integrated studio system amounted to
a monopoly on the American film industry and violated antitrust laws.
7 In 1948, the US Supreme Court issued an antitrust ruling against Paramount Pictures,
ordering Paramount and the other vertically integrated studios to separate their
production and exhibition operations. The ruling triggered major changes in the
American film industry, marking the end of the studio system era and allowing an influx
of independent producers to establish their own operations. Independent production
companies and studio subsidiaries began to work in tandem with major studios to
finance, produce, and distribute movies. Creative talent was free to migrate from movie
to movie without restraint, signing temporary or project-specific deals that did not entail
long-term commitments. This structure continues to form the basis of the Hollywood
system in modern times.
INDEPENDENT FILM
8 The independent filmmaking system has always defied easy definition, but its rise began
in the years following the 1948 Supreme Court ruling that brought about the end of the
golden age of the studio system. During the 1960s, the New Hollywood phenomenon of
independent production reached maturity, posting major critical and commercial
successes with movies like The Graduate (1967) and Midnight Cowboy (1969). During
the 1970s and beyond, the lines between independent film and mainstream film began
to blur, with major studios establishing subsidiary production companies that focused on
smaller, low-budget productions driven by the personal creative visions of producers,
directors, writers, and actors.
9 Some prominent commentators, such as the writer-director John August (1970—), have
argued that independent film is defined more by what a movie is about rather than how
it is financed. August argues that independent movies are not made outside of the
Hollywood system as much as they are made in opposition to its revenue-driven values.
Independent movies have long served to give audiences an alternative and allow for
freer, more creative forms of cinematic expression. They have also helped define
changing tastes and trends from generation to generation and have moved the film
industry forward as a whole.

Explain whether you believe the independent film industry adds value to the American film industry. 
Write a well-organized composition that uses specific evidence from the letter to support your answer.

The independent film industry undeniably adds significant value to the American film industry. While Hollywood traditionally focuses on big-budget, mainstream productions, independent films bring diversity, creativity, and unique storytelling to the cinematic landscape. As highlighted in the text, independent films prioritize artistic vision over mass appeal, allowing for experimentation, exploration of niche themes, and showcasing emerging talent. The success of independent films like "The Graduate" and "Midnight Cowboy" underscores their critical and commercial impact, challenging conventions and pushing the boundaries of storytelling. Additionally, the independent sector serves as a platform for alternative voices, promoting diversity, unique perspectives, and innovative storytelling approaches that enrich and expand the cultural and artistic tapestry of American cinema. Through fostering creativity, challenging norms, and promoting diversity, the independent film industry undoubtedly plays a vital role in adding value to the broader American film industry.
essay style

Independent Films: Enriching the American Cinematic Landscape

The independent film industry undoubtedly contributes immense value to the American film landscape. While Hollywood typically focuses on big-budget blockbusters tailored for mass appeal, independent films bring diversity, creativity, and unique storytelling to the forefront. As detailed in the text, independent films prioritize artistic vision over commercial success, paving the way for experimentation, exploration of niche themes, and the discovery of emerging talent. Notable successes such as "The Graduate" and "Midnight Cowboy" exemplify how independent films can achieve critical acclaim and commercial success while challenging conventions and pushing the boundaries of storytelling. By fostering creativity, embracing alternative perspectives, and providing a platform for diverse voices, the independent film sector plays a pivotal role in enriching and expanding the cultural and artistic tapestry of American cinema. Through its commitment to artistic expression and its ability to offer thought-provoking narratives, the independent film industry stands as a vital component in enhancing the depth and diversity of American filmmaking.

Controlling Idea: Grand Prairie ISD should consider allowing students to observe the eclipse outdoors on April 8, as it presents a rare and valuable educational opportunity that aligns with the school's commitment to hands-on learning experiences, supported by safety precautions outlined in the text.

Grand Prairie ISD should permit students to witness the solar eclipse outdoors on April 8 as it offers a unique and educational experience. The text emphasizes the significance of the eclipse, highlighting the rare chance for students to witness a total solar phenomenon. Allowing students to directly observe this event can foster curiosity and engagement in science and astronomy, aligning with the school's educational objectives. Moreover, the safety precautions outlined in the letter, such as using eclipse glasses and following the stages of the eclipse, ensure that students can witness the event safely while gaining valuable insights into natural phenomena. This hands-on approach to learning can enrich their educational experience and spark a deeper interest in science and exploration. Overall, permitting students to observe the eclipse outdoors provides a valuable learning opportunity that combines education, curiosity, and safety, making it a worthwhile experience for the students at Grand Prairie ISD.
